title: Слово о пълку Игоревѣ
draftNote: Part I — the whole campaign movement (paragraph 2 of the 1800 edition): Igor's resolve, the eclipse, the second invocation of Boyan, the muster and Vsevolod's speech, the setting-out with its omens, and the refrain «O Russian land, beyond the hill.»
source: Text: editio princeps — «Ироическая пѣснь...», ed. A. I. Musin-Pushkin, Moscow, 1800 (public domain); via Russian Wikisource. 1800 orthography preserved. Prose sense-units at the edition's punctuation (no imposed verse lineation). Transliteration: scholarly Slavist romanization (ž š č c x; ě = ѣ; ŭ / ĭ = ъ / ь). Pronunciation: reconstructed late-12th-c. Old East Slavic (see slovo-audio doc); ж/ш/ц read hard by convention. Translations made fresh from the 1800 original.
about: The campaign proper. Prince Igor musters his men and — despite a solar eclipse (the datable eclipse of 1 May 1185, taken as an evil omen) — resolves to march east against the Polovtsians (Cumans) toward the Don. The poet again imagines how the old bard Boyan would have sung it; Igor's brother Vsevolod, 'the Wild Bull,' answers with praise of his hardened warriors of Kursk; the army rides out under ominous signs (the demon-bird Div, the gathering beasts) and crosses beyond the frontier hill — «O Russian land, already you are beyond the hill.» The poem survives only in the 1800 edition (the medieval copy burned in 1812); readings are uncertain and several passages here are famous 'dark places,' flagged in the notes rather than smoothed over.

sentences.t: Почнемъ же, братіе, повѣсть сію отъ стараго Владимера до нынѣшняго Игоря;
sentences.tr: Počnemŭ že, bratie, pověstĭ siju otŭ starago Vladimera do nyněšnjago Igorja;
sentences.l: Let us begin, then, brothers, this tale from old Vladimir to the present Igor;
sentences.i: Let us begin this tale, brothers, from old Vladimir down to the Igor of our own day —
sentences.n: The poet frames the tale's span: «отъ стараго ... до нынѣшняго», from the old to the present. 'Old Vladimir' is debated — most likely Vladimir Monomakh (d. 1125), possibly Vladimir I who baptized Rus'. Почнемъ is a 1st-plural 'let us begin' (the poet drawing the audience in).

sentences.t: иже истягну умь крѣпостію своею, и поостри сердца своего мужествомъ, наплънився ратнаго духа, наведе своя храбрыя плъкы на землю Половѣцькую за землю Руськую.
sentences.tr: iže istjagnu umĭ krěpostiju svojeju, i poostri sĭrdca svojego mužĭstvomŭ, naplŭnivsja ratnago duxa, navede svoja xrabryja plŭky na zemlju Polověcĭkuju za zemlju Rusĭkuju.
sentences.l: who girded his mind with his strength, and sharpened his heart with courage, having filled himself with a warlike spirit, [and] led his brave regiments into the Polovtsian land for the Russian land.
sentences.i: who braced his mind with his own resolve, whetted his heart with valor, and, filled with a warrior's spirit, led his brave regiments into the Polovtsian land for the sake of the Russian land.
sentences.n: A chain of aorists (истягну, поостри, наведе — simple past) narrating Igor's resolve. истягну ('girded / braced / strained') is a rare, debated verb. «за землю Руськую», 'for the Russian land,' sounds the patriotic keynote the poem returns to. плъкы = 'regiments, war-bands' (the пълкъ of the title).

sentences.t: Тогда Игорь възрѣ на свѣтлое солнце и видѣ отъ него тьмою вся своя воя прикрыты, и рече Игорь къ дружинѣ своей:
sentences.tr: Togda Igorĭ vŭzrě na světloe sŭlnce i vidě otŭ nego tĭmoju vsja svoja voja prikryty, i reče Igorĭ kŭ družině svojej:
sentences.l: Then Igor looked up at the bright sun and saw all his warriors covered by darkness from it, and Igor said to his druzhina:
sentences.i: Then Igor looked up at the bright sun and saw all his warriors shrouded in its darkness, and Igor said to his retinue:
sentences.n: The eclipse — the solar eclipse of 1 May 1185, which the chronicles also record; Igor's marching on despite it is the tragic hinge of the poem. воя = 'warriors' (acc. pl.). дружина is the prince's sworn war-band / retinue — keep it as 'druzhina' or 'retinue.'

sentences.t: братіе и дружино! луцежъ бы потяту быти, неже полонену быти:
sentences.tr: bratie i družino! lucežŭ by potjatu byti, neže polonenu byti:
sentences.l: Brothers and druzhina! better it would be to be hewn down than to be taken captive:
sentences.i: 'Brothers and men! Better to be cut down than taken captive —
sentences.n: The heroic maxim: death before capture. луцежъ = лучше + же ('better indeed'); потяту быти 'to be hewn/slain' set against полонену быти 'to be taken prisoner'; неже = 'than.'

sentences.t: а всядемъ, братіе, на свои бръзыя комони, да позримъ синего Дону.
sentences.tr: a vsjademŭ, bratie, na svoi brŭzyja komoni, da pozrimŭ sinego Donu.
sentences.l: so let us mount, brothers, our swift steeds, that we may look upon the blue Don.
sentences.i: so let us mount our swift horses, brothers, and set eyes on the blue Don.'
sentences.n: комони = 'horses' — an old poetic word (beside конь); it recurs for war-horses. «синего Дону», the blue Don, is the goal of the whole march. да + present ('да позримъ') is a purpose clause, 'so that we may look upon.'

sentences.t: Спала Князю умь похоти, и жалость ему знаменіе заступи, искусити Дону великаго.
sentences.tr: Spala Knjazju umĭ poxoti, i žalostĭ jemu znamenie zastupi, iskusiti Donu velikago.
sentences.l: Desire fell upon the prince's mind, and longing blocked the omen for him — to try the great Don.
sentences.i: Longing seized the prince's mind, and his yearning to test the great Don outweighed the omen.
sentences.n: One of the poem's true 'dark places.' «Спала Князю умь похоти» is read roughly as 'desire fell upon / fired the prince's mind,' but the grammar is disputed. «жалость ему знаменіе заступи» = 'longing shut off / overrode the omen for him.' «искусити Дону великаго» = 'to try / put to the test the great Don.' We keep the 1800 wording and mark the uncertainty.

sentences.t: Хощу бо, рече, копіе приломити конець поля Половецкаго съ вами Русици, хощу главу свою приложити, а любо испити шеломомь Дону.
sentences.tr: Xošču bo, reče, kopie prilomiti konĭcĭ polja Poloveckago sŭ vami Rusici, xošču glavu svoju priložiti, a ljubo ispiti šelomomĭ Donu.
sentences.l: For I wish, he said, to break my lance at the edge of the Polovtsian field, together with you, men of Rus'; I wish to lay down my head, or else to drink of the Don from a helmet.
sentences.i: 'For I wish,' he said, 'to break my lance at the far edge of the Polovtsian field, with you, sons of Rus' — to lay down my life, or else drink the Don from my helmet.'
sentences.n: Three heroic idioms: «копіе приломити», to break one's lance = to fight to the finish; «главу свою приложити», to lay down one's head = to die; «испити шеломомь Дону», to drink the Don from a helmet = to reach and master the river — the poem's signature image. Русици = the poet's name for Igor's men.

sentences.t: О Бояне, соловію стараго времени! абы ты сіа плъкы ущекоталъ, скача славію по мыслену древу, летая умомъ подъ облакы, свивая славы оба полы сего времени, рища въ тропу Трояню чресъ поля на горы.
sentences.tr: O Bojane, soloviju starago vremeni! aby ty sia plŭky uščekotalŭ, skača slaviju po myslenu drevu, letaja umomŭ podŭ oblaky, svivaja slavy oba poly sego vremeni, rišča vŭ tropu Trojanju čresŭ polja na gory.
sentences.l: O Boyan, nightingale of the old time! if only you had trilled these hosts [into song], leaping as a nightingale along the tree of thought, flying in mind beneath the clouds, weaving the glories of both halves of this time, coursing along Troyan's path across the fields onto the hills.
sentences.i: O Boyan, nightingale of the old days! Had you but sung these armies — bounding like a nightingale along the tree of thought, soaring in mind beneath the clouds, braiding the glories of both ages together, racing down Troyan's trail across the plains and over the hills —
sentences.n: The poet imagines how Boyan (of the proem) would have sung this campaign. соловію / славію 'nightingale'; «по мыслену древу», 'along the tree of thought,' echoes the proem's disputed «мыслію по древу». «оба полы сего времени» = 'both halves of this time' (past and present). «въ тропу Трояню» — 'on Troyan's trail,' a recurring, obscure reference (a pagan god? the emperor Trajan? an age?) — genuinely uncertain; Троянь is left untranslated and flagged. The imperfective participles (скача, летая, свивая, рища) paint Boyan's soaring manner.

sentences.t: Пѣти было пѣсь Игореви, того (Олга) внуку.
sentences.tr: Pěti bylo pěsĭ Igorevi, togo (Olga) vnuku.
sentences.l: The song was to be sung for Igor, that (Oleg's) grandson.
sentences.i: The song should be sung for Igor, grandson of that Oleg.
sentences.n: «Пѣти было» — 'it was to sing' = 'one ought to sing' (impersonal, infinitive + было). Igor is grandson of Oleg Svyatoslavich ('Gorislavich'), the strife-sowing prince recalled later in the poem. «(Олга)» is bracketed because the word is an editorial supplement. Игореви = dative, 'for Igor.'

sentences.t: Не буря соколы занесе чрезъ поля широкая; галици стады бѣжать къ дону великому; чили въспѣти было вѣщей Бояне, Велесовь внуче!
sentences.tr: Ne burja sokoly zanese čresŭ polja širokaja; galici stady běžatĭ kŭ donu velikomu; čili vŭspěti bylo věščej Bojane, Velesovĭ vnuče!
sentences.l: It was not a storm that bore the falcons across the wide fields; jackdaws in flocks flee toward the great Don; or was it [rather] to be sung, prophetic Boyan, grandson of Veles!
sentences.i: No storm it was that swept the falcons across the wide fields — flocks of jackdaws race toward the great Don. Or should it be sung so, prophetic Boyan, grandson of Veles!
sentences.n: The 'falcons' are Igor's warriors; the storm-that-isn't and the fleeing jackdaws foreshadow disaster. «чили въспѣти было» = 'or was it [rather] to be sung' — the poet weighs Boyan's manner again. «Велесовь внуче», 'grandson of Veles': Veles/Volos, the pagan god of poets and cattle — calling Boyan his grandson marks him as divinely inspired. Another pagan-god reference the app keeps and glosses.

sentences.t: Комони ржуть за Сулою; звенить слава въ Кыевѣ; трубы трубять въ Новѣградѣ; стоять стязи въ Путивлѣ; Игорь ждетъ мила брата Всеволода.
sentences.tr: Komoni ržutĭ za Suloju; zvenitĭ slava vŭ Kyevě; truby trubjatĭ vŭ Nověgradě; stojatĭ stjazi vŭ Putivlě; Igorĭ ždetĭ mila brata Vsevoloda.
sentences.l: Steeds neigh beyond the Sula; glory rings in Kiev; trumpets blare in Novgorod; banners stand in Putivl; Igor awaits his dear brother Vsevolod.
sentences.i: Horses neigh beyond the Sula; fame rings out in Kiev; trumpets sound in Novgorod-Seversk; the standards stand in Putivl — Igor waits for his dear brother Vsevolod.
sentences.n: Five short parallel cola sweep across the campaign's geography — the Sula river, Kiev, Novgorod-Seversky (Igor's seat), Putivl — building to Igor's wait for his brother. «мила брата» = 'dear brother' (acc.). These drumbeat parallel clauses are the poem's signature rhythm.

sentences.t: И рече ему Буй Туръ Всеволодъ: одинъ братъ, одинъ свѣтъ свѣтлый ты Игорю, оба есвѣ Святъславличя;
sentences.tr: I reče jemu Buj Turŭ Vsevolodŭ: odinŭ bratŭ, odinŭ světŭ světlyj ty Igorju, oba jesvě Svjatŭslavličja;
sentences.l: And Buy Tur Vsevolod said to him: one brother, one bright light are you, Igor; both of us are Svyatoslav's sons;
sentences.i: And Buy Tur ('Wild Bull') Vsevolod said to him: 'You are my one brother, my one bright light, Igor; we two are both Svyatoslavichi —
sentences.n: «Буй Туръ» = 'Wild Bull / Furious Aurochs,' Vsevolod's heroic epithet (буй 'wild, furious' + туръ, the aurochs). «оба есвѣ» — 'we both are,' with есвѣ the old DUAL of быти ('are,' of exactly two) — the dual number, alive here and lost in modern Russian; you'll meet it for the two brothers. Святъславличя is likewise dual.

sentences.t: сѣдлай, брате, свои бръзыи комони, а мои ти готови, осѣдлани у Курьска на переди;
sentences.tr: sědlaj, brate, svoi brŭzyi komoni, a moi ti gotovi, osědlani u Kurĭska na peredi;
sentences.l: saddle, brother, your swift steeds — but mine are ready for you, saddled at Kursk, out in front;
sentences.i: saddle your swift horses, brother — mine are already ready for you, saddled ahead at Kursk;
sentences.n: Vsevolod answers Igor's 'let us mount': his own men are already saddled and waiting at Kursk. «а мои ти готови» — 'but mine [are] ready for you' (ти = dative 'for you'). брате = vocative, 'O brother.'

sentences.t: а мои ти Куряни свѣдоми къ мети, подъ трубами повити, подъ шеломы възлелѣяны, конець копія въскръмлени, пути имь вѣдоми, яругы имъ знаеми, луци у нихъ напряжени, тули отворени, сабли изъострени, сами скачють акы сѣрыи влъци въ полѣ, ищучи себе чти, а Князю славѣ.
sentences.tr: a moi ti Kurjani svědomi kŭ meti, podŭ trubami poviti, podŭ šelomy vŭzlelějany, konĭcĭ kopija vŭskrŭmleni, puti imĭ vědomi, jarugy imŭ znaemi, luci u nixŭ naprjaženi, tuli otvoreni, sabli izŭostreni, sami skačjutĭ aky sěryi vlŭci vŭ polě, iščuči sebe čti, a Knjazju slavě.
sentences.l: and my Kurians are seasoned toward the mark: swaddled under war-trumpets, cradled under helmets, fed from the point of a spear; the paths are known to them, the ravines familiar to them, their bows are strung, their quivers open, their sabers sharpened; they themselves leap like grey wolves in the field, seeking honor for themselves, and glory for the prince.
sentences.i: and my men of Kursk are seasoned fighters: swaddled to the sound of war-trumpets, cradled under helmets, weaned on the point of a spear. The trails are known to them, the ravines familiar; their bows are strung, their quivers open, their sabers whetted; they range like grey wolves across the plain, seeking honor for themselves and glory for their prince.
sentences.n: The most famous portrait in the poem: the warriors of Kursk raised for war from the cradle. A pile-up of past-passive participles (повити 'swaddled,' възлелѣяны 'cradled,' въскръмлени 'fed/weaned,' напряжени 'strung,' отворени 'opened,' изъострени 'sharpened') conveys their readiness. «свѣдоми къ мети» is uncertain — roughly 'skilled at the target/aim.' «акы сѣрыи влъци», 'like grey wolves' — the recurring wolf-simile; «чти» = чести 'honor'; the honor-and-glory refrain closes the speech.

sentences.t: Тогда въступи Игорь Князь въ златъ стремень, и поѣха по чистому полю.
sentences.tr: Togda vŭstupi Igorĭ Knjazĭ vŭ zlatŭ stremenĭ, i poěxa po čistomu polju.
sentences.l: Then Igor the prince stepped into the golden stirrup, and rode out over the open field.
sentences.i: Then Prince Igor set his foot in the golden stirrup and rode out across the open plain.
sentences.n: «въступи ... въ златъ стремень» — 'stepped into the golden stirrup,' a set formula for setting out on campaign (it recurs). «чистое поле», 'the open field,' the standard epic expanse. Aorists въступи, поѣха.

sentences.t: Солнце ему тъмою путь заступаше; нощь стонущи ему грозою птичь убуди; свистъ звѣринъ въ стазби; дивъ кличетъ връху древа, велитъ послушати земли незнаемѣ, влъзѣ, и по морію, и по Сулію, и Сурожу, и Корсуню, и тебѣ Тьмутораканьскый блъванъ.
sentences.tr: Sŭlnce jemu tŭmoju putĭ zastupaše; noščĭ stonušči jemu grozoju ptičĭ ubudi; svistŭ zvěrinŭ vŭ stazbi; divŭ kličetĭ vrĭxu dreva, velitĭ poslušati zemli neznaemě, vlŭzě, i po moriju, i po Suliju, i Surožu, i Korsunju, i těbě Tĭmutorakanĭskyj blŭvanŭ.
sentences.l: The sun blocked his way with darkness; the night, groaning to him with a storm, roused the birds; the whistling of beasts arose; Div cries at the top of the tree, bids [them] hearken — to the unknown land, to the Volga, and to the sea-shore, and to the Sula, and to Surozh, and to Korsun, and to you, idol of Tmutarakan.
sentences.i: The sun barred his path with darkness; the groaning night roused the birds with its storm; a whistling of beasts arose. Div calls from the treetop, bidding the unknown lands take heed — the Volga, the seacoast, the Sula, Surozh and Korsun, and you, idol of Tmutarakan.
sentences.n: A dense omen-passage. «дивъ» (Div) — an ominous bird- or demon-creature of Slavic-Iranian lore whose cry warns the foreign lands; left untranslated and glossed. «блъванъ» = a pagan idol / statue (of Tmutarakan). The place-names sweep the whole Cuman/Black-Sea horizon: Влъга (Volga), Сурожъ (Sudak), Корсунь (Cherson), Тьмуторокань. «стазби» is corrupt and uncertain (a 'dark place'). Imperfects (заступаше, убуди, кличетъ) hold the scene in ominous motion.

sentences.t: А Половци неготовами дорогами побѣгоша къ Дону Великому; крычатъ тѣлѣгы полунощы, рци лебеди роспущени.
sentences.tr: A Polovci negotovami dorogami poběgoša kŭ Donu Velikomu; kryčatĭ tělěgy polunoščy, rci lebedi rospuščeni.
sentences.l: And the Polovtsians, by unready roads, ran toward the Great Don; the carts screech at midnight — say, like swans let loose.
sentences.i: And the Polovtsians fled by rough roads toward the Great Don; their carts shriek in the night — like startled swans set loose.
sentences.n: The Cumans flee toward the Don, their wagons screeching. «неготовами дорогами» — 'by unready / rough roads' (instrumental). «рци» = 'say, as it were' (imperative of рещи used to introduce a simile), 'like.' «роспущени», past-passive, 'let loose, scattered.' побѣгоша is an aorist plural, 'they fled.'

sentences.t: Игорь къ Дону вои ведетъ: уже бо бѣды его пасетъ птиць; подобію влъци грозу въ срожатъ, по яругамъ; орли клектомъ на кости звѣри зовутъ, лисици брешутъ на чръленыя щиты.
sentences.tr: Igorĭ kŭ Donu voi vedetĭ: uže bo bědy jego pasetĭ ptičĭ; podobiju vlŭci grozu vŭsrožatĭ, po jarugamŭ; orli klektomĭ na kosti zvěri zovutĭ, lisici brešutĭ na črŭlenyja ščity.
sentences.l: Igor leads his warriors toward the Don: already the birds watch [for] his misfortunes; the wolves stir up dread along the ravines; the eagles with their screaming call the beasts to the bones; the foxes yap at the crimson shields.
sentences.i: Igor leads his men to the Don — and already the birds are on the watch for his ruin: wolves conjure terror along the ravines, eagles with their screeching summon the beasts to the bones, foxes yelp at the crimson shields.
sentences.n: Nature turns predatory before the battle — birds, wolves, eagles, foxes all await the slaughter. «уже бо бѣды его пасетъ птиць» — 'already the bird watches / awaits his misfortunes.' «въ срожатъ» is printed thus in 1800 but is read as one word, въсрожатъ 'they rouse / stir up' (uncertain). «чръленыя щиты» = 'crimson shields' — чрьленъ, the Rus' color, recurs. Present tenses (ведетъ, пасетъ, зовутъ, брешутъ) make the menace immediate.

sentences.t: О руская земле! уже за Шеломянемъ еси.
sentences.tr: O ruskaja zemle! uže za Šelomjanemŭ jesi.
sentences.l: O Russian land! already you are beyond the hill.
sentences.i: O Russian land! Already you are behind the hill.
sentences.n: The poem's great refrain — the army has crossed the Шеломя (a frontier ridge/hill) and left Rus' behind. «за Шеломянемъ еси» — 'you are beyond the Shelomya'; земле is the vocative, 'O land.' Its darker counterpart «уже не Шеломянемъ еси» sounds later, after the defeat.

sentences.t: Длъго. Нось мркнетъ, заря свѣтъ запала, мъгла поля покрыла, щекотъ славій успе, говоръ галичь убуди.
sentences.tr: Dlŭgo. Nosĭ mrknetĭ, zarja světŭ zapala, mŭgla polja pokryla, ščekotŭ slavij uspe, govorŭ galičĭ ubudi.
sentences.l: For long. Night grows dark, the dawn has let the light fall, mist has covered the fields, the trilling of nightingales has fallen asleep, the chatter of jackdaws has awoken.
sentences.i: Long they ride. Night darkens; the afterglow has faded; mist has covered the fields; the nightingales' song has gone to sleep, and the jackdaws' chatter has woken.
sentences.n: «Длъго» ('long / far') is a one-word crux — probably 'for a long time [they marched],' but its attachment is uncertain; we keep it and flag it. Then dusk falls in parallel clauses: as the songbirds hush and the carrion-jackdaws wake, the mood turns to foreboding. «мркнетъ» = 'grows dark' (cf. меркнет); perfects запала, покрыла, успе, убуди.

sentences.t: Русичи великая поля чрьлеными щиты прегородиша, ищучи себѣ чти, а Князю славы.
sentences.tr: Rusiči velikaja polja črŭlenymi ščity pregorodiša, iščuči sebě čti, a Knjazju slavy.
sentences.l: The men of Rus' have barred the great fields with crimson shields, seeking honor for themselves, and glory for the prince.
sentences.i: The men of Rus' have walled off the great plains with their crimson shields, seeking honor for themselves and glory for their prince.
sentences.n: The movement closes on the honor-and-glory refrain (heard of Vsevolod's Kurians and returning throughout): «ищучи себѣ чти, а Князю славы» — 'seeking honor for themselves and glory for the prince.' прегородиша (aorist pl.) 'they walled off, barricaded.' чрьлеными щиты 'with crimson shields,' the Rus' battle-color once more.

glossary.владимера: Владимеръ — Vladimir (gen. sg.); a prince, probably Vladimir Monomakh
glossary.до: до — up to, as far as (prep. + gen.)
glossary.нынѣшняго: нынѣшній — present, of the present day (gen. sg.)
glossary.истягну: истягнути — girded, braced, strained (aorist 3sg; rare, debated)
glossary.наплънився: наплънитися — having filled himself (past participle, reflexive)
glossary.половѣцькую: половѣцькый — Polovtsian, Cuman (acc. sg. fem., adj.)
glossary.за: за — for, for the sake of; behind, beyond (prep.)
glossary.руськую: руськый — Russian, of Rus' (acc. sg. fem., adj.)
glossary.прикрыты: прикрыти — covered, shrouded (past passive participle, acc. pl.)
glossary.потяту: потяти — hewn down, slain (past passive participle, 'to be slain')
glossary.полонену: полонити — taken captive (past passive participle, 'to be captured')
glossary.всядемъ: въсѣсти — let us mount, sit upon (1st pl.)
glossary.комони: комонь — horses, steeds (nom./acc. pl.; poetic for кони)
glossary.позримъ: позьрѣти — let us look upon, behold (1st pl.)
glossary.дону: Донъ — the Don (gen./dat. sg.); the river
glossary.князю: кънязь — to/upon the prince; for the prince (dat. sg.)
glossary.ему: онъ — to him, for him (dat. sg.)
glossary.искусити: искусити — to try, test, put to the test (infinitive)
glossary.хощу: хотѣти — I wish, I want (1st sg.)
glossary.конець: коньць — end, edge (acc. sg., 'at the edge of')
glossary.поля: поле — field, plain (gen. sg.; also acc. pl. 'fields')
glossary.русици: Русичь — sons of Rus', men of Rus' (voc./nom. pl.)
glossary.приложити: приложити — to lay down, lay alongside (infinitive)
glossary.умомъ: умъ — in mind, with the mind (instr. sg.)
glossary.въ: въ — in, into; (here) part of въсрожатъ (prep.)
glossary.трояню: Троянь — Troyan's (possessive adj.); an obscure mythic/historical name
glossary.было: быти — was, ought (impersonal past; with infinitive 'one ought to')
glossary.олга: Олегъ — Oleg's (gen. sg.); Igor's grandfather Oleg Svyatoslavich
glossary.велесовь: Велесовъ — Veles's (possessive adj.); of the god Veles/Volos
glossary.сулою: Сула — the Sula (instr. sg.); a river
glossary.туръ: туръ — aurochs, wild bull (nom. sg.); Vsevolod's epithet
glossary.есвѣ: быти — we two are (1st dual present); the dual number
glossary.ти: ты — for you, to you (dat. sg., enclitic)
glossary.осѣдлани: осѣдлати — saddled (past passive participle, nom. pl.)
glossary.куряни: Курянинъ — men of Kursk, Kurians (nom. pl.)
glossary.мети: мета — the mark, target, aim (dat. sg.); uncertain reading
glossary.повити: повити — swaddled (past passive participle, nom. pl.)
glossary.възлелѣяны: възлелѣяти — cradled, nurtured (past passive participle, nom. pl.)
glossary.въскръмлени: въскръмити — fed, weaned (past passive participle, nom. pl.)
glossary.напряжени: напрящи — strung, drawn taut (past passive participle, nom. pl.)
glossary.отворени: отворити — opened (past passive participle, nom. pl.)
glossary.изъострени: изъострити — sharpened, whetted (past passive participle, nom. pl.)
glossary.чистому: чистый — clear, open (dat. sg., 'over the open')
glossary.грозою: гроза — with a storm, with terror (instr. sg.)
glossary.звѣринъ: звѣринъ — of beasts, bestial (nom. sg., adj.)
glossary.стазби: — (a corrupt, uncertain word: 'in the thicket'? — a dark place)
glossary.дивъ: дивъ — Div (nom. sg.); an ominous demon-bird of Slavic-Iranian lore
glossary.връху: връхъ — at the top of (loc. sg. + gen.)
glossary.влъзѣ: Влъга — to the Volga (dat. sg.); the river
glossary.морію: море — to the sea, seashore (dat. sg.)
glossary.сулію: Сулій / Сула — to the Sula (dat. sg.)
glossary.сурожу: Сурожъ — to Surozh (dat. sg.); Sudak, in Crimea
glossary.корсуню: Корсунь — to Korsun (dat. sg.); Cherson, in Crimea
glossary.рци: рещи — say! (imperative; here 'as it were, like')
glossary.роспущени: роспустити — let loose, scattered (past passive participle, nom. pl.)
glossary.подобію: — (uncertain: 'in a like manner'? — with the wolves)
glossary.срожатъ: въсрожати — rouse, stir up (3rd pl.; въ срожатъ read as въсрожатъ, uncertain)
glossary.клектомъ: клектъ — with screaming, with a scream (instr. sg.)
glossary.шеломянемъ: шеломя — the frontier hill / ridge (instr. sg.)
glossary.длъго: длъго — long, for a long time (adv.); a crux
glossary.нось: нощь — night (nom. sg.; variant of нощь)
glossary.запала: запасти — has set, let fall (past 3sg fem.)
glossary.русичи: Русичь — the men of Rus' (nom. pl.)
